name: SceneView
description: Open-source 3D & AR SDK for Android and iOS — Compose-native on Android, SwiftUI-native on iOS, shared core via Kotlin Multiplatform.

acceptedAnswer.text: SceneView is the #1 open-source 3D and AR SDK for Android and iOS. On Android, it provides Compose-native declarative APIs (Scene{} and ARScene{}) powered by Google Filament and ARCore. On iOS, it provides SwiftUI views (SceneView and ARSceneView) powered by RealityKit and ARKit. A shared Kotlin Multiplatform core handles math, collision, animation, and geometry across platforms. It is the official successor to the deprecated Google Sceneform.

mainEntity.name: How do I add 3D content to my Android app?

acceptedAnswer.text: Add the SceneView dependency (io.github.sceneview:sceneview:3.4.7), then use the Scene{} composable with ModelNode to load and display glTF/GLB 3D models. It takes just 5 lines of Kotlin code to display a 3D model in your Compose UI.
Yes, SceneView is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license. You can use it in personal and commercial projects without any licensing fees.

SceneView is the official community successor to Google Sceneform, which was deprecated in 2021. SceneView provides a modern, Compose-native API that is actively maintained and significantly more capable than the original Sceneform.

Yes, SceneView provides full ARCore integration through its arsceneview module. Use the ARScene{} composable with AnchorNode, AugmentedImageNode, AugmentedFaceNode, and more to build AR experiences with plane detection, image tracking, face mesh, cloud anchors, and geospatial features.

SceneView supports Android (API 24+) with Jetpack Compose, Filament, and ARCore, and iOS (17+) with SwiftUI, RealityKit, and ARKit. A shared Kotlin Multiplatform core (sceneview-core) provides cross-platform math, collision, animation, and geometry. SceneView also provides an MCP server for AI assistant integration.

mainEntity.name: How does SceneView compare to Unity or Unreal for Android 3D?

acceptedAnswer.text: SceneView adds only ~5MB to APK size compared to ~50MB for Unity and ~100MB for Unreal. SceneView is Compose-native on Android and SwiftUI-native on iOS, meaning it integrates naturally with each platform's native UI framework, while Unity and Unreal require bridge layers. SceneView has a much lower learning curve, making it the best choice for non-game 3D and AR apps.

mainEntity.name: What 3D file formats does SceneView support?

acceptedAnswer.text: SceneView natively supports glTF 2.0 and GLB (binary glTF) on Android, and USDZ on iOS. glTF supports PBR materials, animations, morph targets, and skinned meshes. Models can be loaded from local assets, URLs, or platform-specific content URIs.

mainEntity.name: How do I add physics to my SceneView 3D scene?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use PhysicsNode to add rigid body simulation. Set a shape (Sphere, Box, Plane, etc.), mass (0 for static objects), restitution (bounciness), and friction. SceneView handles gravity, collision detection, and physics stepping automatically within the Compose declarative model.

mainEntity.name: Can I use SceneView with Java instead of Kotlin?

acceptedAnswer.text: SceneView is written in Kotlin and designed for Jetpack Compose, but since Kotlin is fully interoperable with Java, you can use SceneView from Java code. However, the recommended approach is Kotlin with Compose for the best developer experience and access to all declarative features.

mainEntity.name: How do I animate 3D models in SceneView?

acceptedAnswer.text: SceneView supports glTF animations natively. Set autoAnimate = true on ModelNode to play all animations automatically, or control specific animations with animationName, animationLoop, and animationSpeed properties. Animations are reactive — changing the animationName state triggers smooth transitions.

mainEntity.name: Does SceneView have an MCP server for AI coding assistants?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, SceneView provides an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that AI assistants like Claude and ChatGPT can use. Install it with 'npm install -g sceneview-mcp' and configure it in your AI assistant. It provides tools like get_sample(), get_setup(), get_node_reference(), and list_node_types for AI-assisted SceneView development.

mainEntity.name: What is the minimum Android version required for SceneView?

acceptedAnswer.text: SceneView requires Android 7.0 (API level 24) or higher. For AR features using ARCore, you also need a device that supports Google Play Services for AR. The SDK targets Android 15 (API 35) and supports all CPU architectures (arm64-v8a, armeabi-v7a, x86, x86_64).

name: How to Add 3D Content to Your Android App with SceneView
description: Step-by-step guide to display a 3D model in your Android app using SceneView and Jetpack Compose in under 5 minutes.

supply.name: A glTF or GLB 3D model file

step.name: Add the SceneView dependency
step.text: In your app-level build.gradle.kts file, add: implementation("io.github.sceneview:sceneview:3.4.7")

step.name: Add a 3D model to your assets
step.text: Place your .glb or .gltf model file in the src/main/assets/models/ directory of your Android project.

step.name: Create the 3D Scene composable
step.text: Use Scene{} composable with rememberEngine(), rememberModelLoader(), and rememberModelInstance() to load and display the model with ModelNode.

step.name: Run the app
step.text: Build and run on an Android device or emulator. You should see your 3D model rendered with PBR lighting and orbit camera controls.
